opus_reasoning: No angels appear; the supernatural element is a dead man's voice/spirit, not a malakh.
Elijah the Prophet appears studying with Rabbi Chaim and speaks to him, functioning as an angelic/heavenly messenger figure explicitly testifying about the Rema.
The story mentions 'הדומה למלאך' (compared to an angel) as metaphor for the rebbe, but no angels actually appear.
The story features demonic/harmful spirits (מחבלים, חיצונים) and dream-figures, not angels (מלאכים).
The story mentions a heavenly court (פמליא של מעלה) but no actual angels appear.
No angels appear; the supernatural element is a dead man's spirit, not an angel.
Elijah the Prophet appears and speaks in the story, functioning as an angelic/heavenly messenger figure.
No angels appear; the story mentions heavenly court (pamalia shel ma'ala) but not מלאכים specifically.
Elijah the prophet is mentioned as revealing himself to the brother, but no angels (מלאכים) appear in the story.
Story features demonic/klipot tormentors and a tzaddik's rescue, but no explicit angels (מלאכים) appear.
The story describes Baal Shem Tov's intense prayer and mentions the Samekh-Mem (Satan/adversary) intercepting prayers, but no שדים/מזיקים (demons/evil spirits) appear.
The story mentions Sama'el (Satan) intercepting prayers, but no שדים/מזיקים (demons/evil spirits) per se appear.
The Samael (ס״מ), a demonic figure, attempts to snatch the preacher and the Besht rebukes him.
The story explicitly identifies the captain who rescued the Besht as Elijah the prophet.
Elijah the prophet does not appear in this story about the Baal Shem Tov.
Story features the Baal Shem Tov and a converted bishop; Elijah the prophet does not appear.
Elijah is mentioned only as a newly-added biblical figure in authority DB context; the story itself does not depict Elijah as a character.
The story features the Baal Shem Tov and a converted bishop; Elijah the prophet does not appear.
Elijah the prophet does not appear in this story about R. Zusha and R. Elimelech.
Elijah explicitly appears to the Besht's father in the story: 'נגלה אליו אליהו ז"ל' and prophesies the birth of his son.
Elijah is mentioned only in the name 'Israel ben Eliezer' (the Besht's father); the prophet Elijah does not appear as a character.
The story features the Baal Shem Tov and Jonah son of Amittai, not Elijah the prophet.
The Besht travels incognito, concealing his identity from the innkeeper and posing as a mere preacher, fitting the hidden-righteous motif.
The arendar is a pious, blessed man but not depicted as a concealed/nistar tzaddik archetype; he openly conducts business and the story emphasizes bitachon, not hiddenness.
The story features the Besht, R. Yaakov, and a repentant bishop; no hidden righteous/nistar/lamed-vavnik figure appears.
Story features named tzaddikim (Maggid of Kozhnitz, Seer of Lublin), not hidden righteous figures.
The story features the Baal Shem Tov, his disciple, and a converted bishop—no hidden righteous/lamed-vavnik figure appears.
Story involves the Baal Shem Tov and reincarnation themes, but no hidden righteous person/nistar/lamed-vavnik character appears.
Story features the Besht and his disciple openly as known tzaddikim; no hidden/nistar character.
The rabbi (R. Leib Sarah's) is a known revealed tzaddik acting openly, not a hidden/nistar figure.
Story about R. Shneur Zalman of Liadi's arrest; no hidden righteous/lamed-vavnik figure depicted.
Story features a known tzaddik (Admor of Strashelye) saving a man from demons; no hidden righteous/nistar/lamed-vavnik figure appears.
The story features Rabbi Elimelech of Lizhensk, an openly known tzaddik, not a hidden/nistar righteous figure.
The story features a known tzaddik (R. Hirsch Leib) and his disciple, but no hidden righteous/nistar figure.
The tzaddik of Sasov is openly known and sought; no hidden/nistar righteous figure appears.
Story features known tzaddikim (Maggid of Kozhnitz, Seer of Lublin), not hidden righteous figures.
The story features named tzaddikim (R. Elimelech, R. Zusha) and a parable about a king's servant; no hidden/nistar righteous figure appears.
The young avrekh is sent by his rebbe as a hidden agent whose Torah words secretly penetrate the innkeeper's heart, functioning as a concealed righteous emissary.
The story explicitly discusses whether the wonder-working innkeeper is one of the 'צדיקים נסתרים' (hidden righteous), a central theme.
No hidden righteous/nistar/lamed-vavnik figure appears; the Besht and other tzaddikim are openly known.
The story features a revealed tzaddik (the Maharal/Gur Aryeh) openly known as rabbi of Prague, not a hidden righteous person.
The story features the Besht and his disciple R. David Pirkus openly as known tzaddikim, with no hidden/nistar righteous figure.
The tzaddik is a famous, publicly renowned rabbi/advisor to the Kaiser, not a hidden righteous person.
Story features R. Elimelech of Lizensk, a famous revealed tzaddik, not a hidden righteous figure.
The story features a pious Hasid and the Apter Rebbe, but no hidden righteous/nistar/lamed-vavnik figure is depicted.
Story concerns named tzaddikim (R. Avraham, the Maggid, etc.) and a prophetic widow; no hidden/nistar righteous figure.
The story features a known tzaddik (the Sabba of Spola), not a hidden/nistar righteous figure.
Story explicitly features a non-Jewish מכשפה who uses a שד to perform כשפים.
No maggid/preacher character appears in the story.
The maggid R. Leib of Polnoy is sent by the Besht to preach in Slutsk, appearing as a distinct preacher character.
The story features 'הרב המוכיח' (the preacher/maggid) as a distinct character interacting with R. Nachman.
The story features R. Yoel, maggid (מ"מ = מגיד מישרים) of Nemirov, as a central character distinct from the Besht.
The Maggid of Mezeritch is explicitly referenced as a distinct character (הרב המגיד הגדול ממעזריטש) whose teachings Shneur Zalman traveled to hear.
The Maggid of Mezritch is a central character distinct from the rebbe figures, and the story repeatedly references him as 'המגיד הגדול'.
The word 'maggidim' appears only as an insult ('חסידים ומגידים'), not as a depicted preacher character.
No maggid/preacher character appears in the story.
The Maggid of Mezritch (הרב המגיד) appears repeatedly as a distinct character — the teacher of the protagonist.
The story explicitly references 'הרב המגיד הגדול דמעזריטש' (the Great Maggid of Mezeritch) as a distinct figure.
The story references המגיד הגדול (the Great Maggid) who wrote a letter of recommendation for R. Leib Sarah's.
The story mentions R' David serving as a shochet in Teltshin ('בהיותו שוחט בק"ק טלטשין').
R. Zelig lodges at a shochet's house in the village of Ispoli.
The Besht commands a sofer to write protective amulets (shemirot).
The tzaddikim's own family members do not appear; the story concerns a childless chasid and his wife/former fiancée, not the tzaddik's family.
No immediate family members of the tzaddik appear; the story centers on the tzaddik and his friend/colleague.
The tzaddik (Besht) appears, but no family members of his are depicted.
No immediate family members of a tzaddik appear as characters; the story features the Besht with a wealthy visitor, not his family.
No family members of the tzaddik appear; the story involves the tzaddik (R. Leib Sarah's) and an avrekh/bride, not the tzaddik's family.
The tzaddik Rabbi Baruch of Mezhibuzh is identified as grandson of the Baal Shem Tov, invoking a family relationship.
No immediate family members of the tzaddik appear; story features the rebbe and lay followers.
No family members of the tsaddik (Elimelech) appear; the story focuses on the tsaddik, a Jewish leaseholder, and the Polish nobleman and his wife.
No immediate family members of the tzaddik appear; the story involves the tzaddik, his disciple, and an innkeeper.
The story features two tzaddikim and a childless chasid, but no family members of the tzaddikim appear as characters.
No immediate family members of a tsaddik appear; characters are the tsaddik and his friend/colleague.
No immediate family members of the tsaddik appear; characters are the tsaddik, his disciple, and the innkeeper.
The Besht's wife (הרבנית) is mentioned only incidentally in a recollection about scratching the maid's scabs; she does not appear as an acting character in the story.
No family members of the tzaddik (Besht or others) appear as characters.
The tzaddik (Maharal) appears but no members of his immediate family are depicted; the family shown is the boy's father, not the tzaddik's.
The story features R. David Pirkus's daughter (the bride) as a central plot element driving the action.
The tsaddik's own family members do not appear; the daughter/father are the gvir's family, not the tsaddik's.
No family members of the tzaddik (Elimelech) appear; the story centers on the tzaddik, the lord, his wife, and the Jewish leaseholder.
The story centers on Reb Yeshaya the Hasid and his daughters, not on the tzaddik (Apter Rav) whose family does not appear.
The family depicted (mother and sons) belongs to other figures, not to the tzaddik Besht himself.
The tzaddik (Besht) appears, but no members of his immediate family are characters; the wife and son in the story belong to the petitioner, not the tzaddik.
No immediate family members of a tzaddik appear as active characters; mentions of fathers-in-law, sons, etc. are incidental and not the tzaddik's own family.
No family members of the tzaddik appear; the story involves the rebbe, an avrekh, and a wealthy bride.
The tzaddik (Sava of Spola) appears alone; no family members are depicted.
The story involves a deceased wife's jealousy and oath about her niece marrying her widower, but no adultery occurs or is at issue.
The story contains no depiction or discussion of adultery.
Story concerns debt, gilgul, and rebuke for ungenerosity; no adultery.
The story explicitly describes the widow's promiscuity (זנתה עם כמה פרוצים) after her husband's death, which constitutes adultery/sexual transgression central to the plot.
Story concerns trust in God, a marriage match, and a robbery/vow — no adultery.
Story concerns hospitality (hachnasat orchim), childbirth via blessing, and a foolish son; no adultery.
Story concerns debt, reincarnation, and stinginess; no adultery.
Story concerns trust in tzaddik, robbery, and a vow of charity; no adultery.
The story tests the wife's faithfulness and she explicitly refuses; no adultery occurs.
Story is about faith, matchmaking, and miraculous wealth; no adultery.
No adultery depicted in the story.
The story centers on joy, laughter, and Sabbath delight; no character displays anger.
The Besht rebukes/scolds (ויגער) the bride and the dead wife's spirit, depicting moments of anger/rebuke.
The story explicitly describes anger multiple times: the wife's wrath ('בערה בה חמתה'), the husband's fury ('אערה עליו את כל חמתי'), and the rabbi rebuking with anger ('ויתן הרב עיניו בו בכעס').
The lord becomes enraged at the informer and beats him severely — a clear depiction of anger.
No character displays anger; story emphasizes humility and weeping, not wrath.
R' Nachman explicitly הקפיד (was angry/took offense) at the Mokhiach, and the Mokhiach's own הקפיד is also mentioned.
No anger is depicted; the story centers on joy, Sabbath delight, and laughter.
The rich man rebukes/scolds the poor beggar and strikes him, but the narrative frames this as cruelty/stinginess rather than emphasizing anger as such; anger is not a focal theme.
No character is depicted as angry; the robbers are murderous and greedy but not described in terms of anger as a moral/emotional moment.
Story is about trust in God (bitachon); no anger depicted.
The Vilna Gaon is described as angry/writing harshly against the Hasidim ('חרה מאוד', 'כתב עליהם שטנה מאד'), and Avigdor's vindictive anger drives the persecution.
No anger depicted; emotions shown are sadness, humility, and joy.
The king's displeasure with his son is brief and functional to the parable; no significant depiction of anger as a moral/emotional focus.
No character is depicted as angry; the robbers are greedy/murderous but anger as such is not portrayed.
No character displays anger; the story centers on faith, trust, and a miraculous match.
The rabbi explicitly becomes angry (ויקצוף הרב מאד, וירגז הרב) at the Besht over tefillin and the mezuzah.
The story explicitly states the lord became angry (וכעס עליו האדון) and imprisoned the man's son.
The merchant becomes 'very angry' (וירגז מאד) at the host briefly upon seeing his goods, but it's a minor incidental moment, not a thematic depiction of anger as moral failing.
The Tsar's anger at finding the Rabbi in Petersburg ('חרה להקייסר') and the informer Avigdor's anger at the Hasidim ('חרה אפו על עדת החסידים') are explicitly depicted.
Story describes disputes and opposition but no character is explicitly depicted as angry.
Story narrates the Rebbe's flight from Napoleon and his death; no depiction of a character's anger.
R. Israel becomes angry (וחרה לו) at R. Azriel for sleeping instead of working.
R. Shalom gazes in awe at the Sabba Kadisha's dance, recognizing the holiness of his every step — depicts awe of a tzaddik.
The story depicts awe of the tzaddik—R. Shalom's astonishment at R. Uri of Strelisk's holiness and ruach hakodesh, leading him to accept him as rebbe.
The story explicitly describes the scholar as 'ירא שמים' and his wife as 'יראה השם', and depicts trembling/awe ('ויחרד מאוד') before the Baal Shem Tov's revelation.
The 200 gold coins are given to fulfill a personal appeasement/dowry obligation to the wronged fiancée's brother, not as charity to the poor.
Story involves mystical writings, the Besht's origins, and supernatural events; no depiction of charity/almsgiving to the poor.
Story concerns R. Shneur Zalman's imprisonment and disputes; no depiction of charity to the poor (the fundraising is for legal defense, not tzedakah).

Story involves loans, restitution, and aid to the dispossessed Jew, but no almsgiving to the poor as charity (tzedakah) is depicted; the begging scenes are about the nobleman's suffering, not Jewish charity.
The 200 gold coins are given as appeasement/dowry to fulfill a pledge to the former fiancée, not as charity to the poor.
Story concerns idolatry, rescue, and prayer; no depiction of charity/almsgiving.
Story concerns a fraudulent get and marriage intrigue; no almsgiving or provision for the poor is depicted.
Story is about a tzaddik's miraculous rescue via confession and a peasant; no almsgiving depicted.
Story is about faith in tzaddikim and divine retribution; loans/restoration of property are not charity to the poor.
Story contains feasts, mystical events, and robbers but no depiction of almsgiving or providing for the poor.
Story concerns demonic affliction and rescue by the rebbe; no almsgiving or provision for the poor depicted.
Story is about a rebbe arranging a match via a loan/business scheme; no almsgiving to the poor is depicted.
The townspeople trembled (ויחרדו) at the Besht's arrival, and Shabtai's wife feared/worried about Shabbat provisions — depicting fear.
Story involves danger (robbers) but no explicit fear language; Besht acts with mesirut nefesh, not fear.
The informer is explicitly afraid to walk alone (היה מתיירא לילך יחידי) and the story depicts his fear/repentance and torment.
Story features sadness, despair, and self-sacrifice, but no explicit fear/פחד/יראה of God, tzaddik, or danger is depicted.
Story features wonder and grief but no explicit depiction of fear (פחד/חרדה) of God, tzaddik, or danger.
Story explicitly depicts fear: 'אל תפחד', 'לב בעל הברית פחד', 'הוא ירא ממנו', 'בפחי נפש... כי ירא מאוד'.
The women fear the demons in the synagogue (מפחדים) and flee.
No depiction of fear; the townspeople's 'trembling' (ויחרדו) at the Besht's arrival is incidental excitement/awe, not a fear theme.
The Besht weeps over potential Sabbath desecration, and R' Michel is described as 'ירא' (afraid) to distress the rabbi.
Judges fearing the powerful man is mentioned briefly, but fear is not a depicted experience central to the story.
No explicit fear depicted; the woman repents and weeps but fear of God/danger is not the focus.
Story features distress, worry, and pleading for life, but no explicit fear (פחד/יראה) of God, tzaddik, or danger as a thematic element.
Story centers on bitachon (trust in God); no depiction of fear of God, tzaddik, or danger.
The child stands 'בפחי נפש' fearful with no helper, and is beaten until forced in fear into the carcass.
The story explicitly depicts fear — the Rebbe of Lublin's fear of being weakened by the Rebbe of Mezhibuzh, and at the end Reb Tzvi was frightened (הפחיד) by his master's stern warning.

The Baal Shem Tov's mysterious laughter is retrospectively explained/recognized as joy over R' Shabtai's Shabbat dancing, and the humble bookbinder is revealed to be a righteous man worthy of fathering the Maggid of Kozhnitz.
The story ends with the retrospective recognition that the captain who rescued them was actually Elijah the Prophet.
The bride recognizes the Baal Shem Tov's voice as the one who saved her from death, and the wicked lord retrospectively realizes who his adversary was.
The rabbi reveals at the end that the merchant who gave him money was actually an angel created from his act of saving lives — a retrospective recognition of the stranger's true identity.
The wealthy host is retrospectively recognized as the former bishop whom the Baal Shem Tov converted, a classic belated identity revelation.
The townspeople belatedly recognize the disguised wanderer as the famed R. David, and the true sinner is revealed to be the Darshan, both classic retrospective recognitions.
The brother belatedly recognizes that the woman who appeared at the fair was his deceased sister sent from heaven — a clear retrospective recognition of hidden identity/meaning.
The rabbi (and townspeople) only learn after the informer's death—via a dream-question—that he had repented, withstood torture, and saved a woman, a clear belated recognition of his righteousness.

Rabbi Moshe Isserles belatedly recognizes that the alleged sinner brought before him is actually his hidden-righteous friend R. Chaim, and further learns that R. Chaim's concealed humility surpasses his own stature.
The king retrospectively recognizes that his advisor's wisdom comes from divine/supernatural source, and the wife later learns her husband is actually a Jew — clear belated recognitions.

Multiple retrospective recognitions: Besht's son recognizes Israel as the destined recipient of the writings; townspeople come to recognize him as a man of God; robbers realize his holiness after the mountains miraculously level.
R' Gershon and others belatedly recognize the Besht's true greatness, having previously considered him an ignoramus.
The host belatedly realizes his guest is the holy Baal Shem Tov ('ונתגלה אז הבעש״ט'), a classic retrospective recognition.
The scholar belatedly recognizes the Baal Shem Tov's true greatness, realizing the dream was real divine revelation, and becomes his devoted follower.
The wealthy host is retrospectively revealed to be the former bishop from R' Yaakov's story, a classic belated recognition of hidden identity.

The townspeople belatedly recognize R. David as the famed tzaddik (after two witnesses identify him), and the community realizes the darshan was actually the sinner — classic retrospective recognition.

The Baal Shem Tov's companions belatedly learn the meaning of his mysterious laughter, and the townspeople discover that the 'simple' R' Shabtai is actually a hidden righteous man whose joy caused heaven to dance.
The story depicts mystical revelation and martyrdom but does not center on a character belatedly recognizing another's true identity or hidden righteousness.

The wealthy man from Risha belatedly recognizes, through the Baal Shem Tov's tale, that he himself is the protagonist and that the beggar he killed was his former friend reincarnated — a clear retrospective recognition.
The story features prophetic revelation by the Baal Shem Tov and trust in God, but no character belatedly recognizes another's true identity or hidden nature.
The rabbi reveals at the end that the merchant who gave him money was actually an angel created from his mitzvah — a clear retrospective recognition of the true nature of an earlier encounter.
The husband belatedly recognizes the old man as the tzaddik R. Leib Sarah's, and the couple realize they were shaped by his hidden hand.
The story depicts a rebuke and severance after mocking tzaddikim, not a belated realization of someone's true identity or hidden nature.
The emissaries and reader belatedly learn the hidden righteousness/spiritual power of the drunkard through the rebbe's revelation.
The rabbi and townspeople belatedly learn via dream-revelation that the despised informer had repented and died righteous — a clear retrospective recognition.

The story features supernatural rescue and recognition of the tzaddik's power, but no character belatedly realizes a hidden identity or truth about another person; the man knew all along who saved him.
R' Shalom belatedly recognizes the Sabba Kadisha's holiness, declaring that his every step effects yichudim — a retrospective recognition of hidden righteousness.
The nobleman belatedly realizes his suffering was punishment for expelling the Jewish leaseholder, recognizing the tzaddik's power and God's justice.
R' Yisrael belatedly recognizes that the sleeping worker R' Azriel is actually a hidden tzaddik praying secretly at night, a clear retrospective recognition.

The rebbe retrospectively reveals the hidden meaning: the innkeeper was a great sinner, the dead children were his mamzerim, and the avrekh's mission was to bring him to repentance — classic retrospective recognition.
The man belatedly realizes the woman he met at the fair was actually his deceased former fiancée sent from heaven — a clear retrospective recognition.

Rabbi Moshe Isserles belatedly recognizes that the supposed sinner brought before him is actually his hidden-righteous friend R. Chaim, and further learns that Chaim has surpassed him spiritually and merits Elijah's revelation.
The wealthy host belatedly recognizes that his sleeping guest is a holy man, and the Besht's identity is revealed (ונתגלה אז הבעש״ט) to the community after the decree is averted.
The emissaries and reader belatedly learn that the despised drunkard is actually a hidden righteous figure whose past mitzvah granted him decree-power, a classic retrospective recognition.

The rebbe retrospectively reveals the hidden meaning: the innkeeper's death, the two gentile children killed nearby were actually his mamzer offspring, and his repentance's cosmic significance — all belatedly recognized.
The rabbi belatedly recognizes the beggar boy as the son of the hospitable old man whom he had blessed years earlier.
The Baal Shem Tov belatedly recognizes that the boorish host is actually a hidden tzaddik and the woman is his former servant Sarah, revealing the hidden meaning of the entire Sabbath ordeal.
The wealthy man from Risha belatedly realizes that the beggar he killed was his former friend reincarnated to collect a debt, a clear retrospective recognition of hidden meaning.
The boy belatedly realizes the supposed in-laws are actually idolatrous priests who lured him as a sacrifice — a clear retrospective recognition of hidden identity/situation.

The emissaries discover the first 'ilui' is actually playing with an idol, retrospectively recognizing the tzaddik's warning was prophetic; also the merchant realizes his rescuer's identity and his unfulfilled vow.
The family and bride belatedly recognize the arriving stranger as the true husband, exposing the messenger's fraud — a clear retrospective recognition.
The nobleman belatedly recognizes his sin against the Jewish leaseholder and the hidden meaning of his suffering, and realizes a spirit had replaced him for a year.

The rich Isaiah eventually realizes the matchmaker meant his poor nephew (same name), and the wealthy father-in-law belatedly learns the truth about his counterpart's poverty — clear retrospective recognition.
The king retrospectively recognizes that the minister's advice must come from a divine/magical source, forcing him to reveal the Jew's true identity; also the wife learns her husband is a Jew.

Multiple retrospective recognitions occur: the Besht's son recognizes Israel as the destined recipient of the writings; townspeople later recognize him as a man of God; Rabbi Gershon's sister accepts the hidden righteousness of the disguised Besht.
The story depicts the Besht's hidden righteousness being revealed — his wife knew but didn't tell, and the madwoman publicly recognizes/reveals his true spiritual stature.
The maskil belatedly recognizes that his dream was real revelation from the Besht and that the Besht is a true tzaddik, leading to his becoming a complete tzaddik.
The story contains retrospective recognition — the tzaddikim are ashamed when they realize the woman saw/foresaw better than they did regarding not traveling to Chvastov.

The rabbi recognizes the preacher as the Besht's disciple via a dream-vision, and recalls his uncle's deathbed prophecy identifying 'Israel ben the midwife' as the true tzaddik — a retrospective recognition.
The story depicts mourning, mystical revelation, and explanation of a decree, but no character belatedly recognizes the true identity or nature of another character.
The servant and others belatedly realize R' Leib's hidden activities, and the disciples retrospectively recognize the porter was a dead soul and understand the purpose of R' Leib's fair-travels.
The wife belatedly reveals to her husband that the visitor was the tzaddik R. Leib Sarah's, and both retrospectively recognize how he orchestrated their match.
The story shows rebuke and excommunication for mocking tzaddikim, but no character belatedly realizes a hidden truth about another.

The story depicts the Maggid's foresight and a sweetening of judgments via humiliation, but no discrete supernatural miracle event with clear before/after intervention on a recipient.
The story depicts conflict, clairvoyant knowledge (pasul tefillin), and supernatural awe, but no discrete miracle with clear before/after rescue or transformation of a recipient.

The story depicts a discrete miracle: the Rebbe's instructions save the man from demonic forces, and later he revives from death after 24 hours — clear before/after supernatural intervention attached to the recipient.
Story depicts visions, spiritual journeys, and tzaddikim recognizing each other, but no discrete miracle event with clear before/after intervention on a recipient.

Parable/teaching about devotion; no discrete miraculous event depicted.
Discrete supernatural events: the two mamzer children are struck dead by falling logs as a heavenly decree to allow the penitent's soul to ascend, with clear before/after.

Story features prophetic dreams and visitations from the deceased, but no discrete miracle (נס/מופת) with a clear before/after intervention.
The Besht perceives a cosmic accusation and manipulates a priest to nullify it; no discrete miracle with before/after for a recipient.
Historical/biographical narrative about disputes; no discrete miracle event depicted.
Discrete miracles: the soldier falls from carriage upon seeing the Rav's tefillin and breaks his leg; wagon wheels break to prevent Sabbath desecration.
Political/communal narrative about a spy among the Mitnagdim; no discrete supernatural miracle depicted.
Story explicitly depicts discrete miraculous releases from prison (נס) tied to Psalm verse 'פדה בשלום נפשי' and other deliverances commemorated as miracles.

The story features supernatural travel/hidden tzaddikim and a wandering dead soul, but no discrete miracle event with clear before/after benefit to a recipient.
The tzaddik orchestrates a match through foresight and arrangement; no discrete supernatural miracle event with before/after is depicted.

Story features mystical visions, prophetic recognition, and saintly perception, but no discrete miracle event with clear before/after intervention for a recipient.

The Baal Shem Tov perceives Elijah and his teacher standing beside him, invisible to others — clear supernatural perception.
The Or HaChaim recognizes the Baal Shem Tov from seeing him in the Heavenly Palace of Messiah, and the Besht knows of Reb Gershon's imprisonment supernaturally — clear ruach hakodesh perception.
The boy's soul ascends and perceives the heavenly court, books of sins/merits, and the interventions of the tzaddikim — clear supernatural perception.
The rabbi supernaturally knows the lost promissory note is in the salt barrel and that it has miraculously blessed the salt.
R. Shalom perceives with 'eina pekicha' that the Saba's footsteps effect mystical yichudim — supernatural perception.
The Rebbe of Lublin knows of the man's broken betrothal and the deceased fiancée's appearance at the fair through ruach hakodesh; the Maggid of Kozhnitz also perceives supernaturally.
The widow repeatedly receives prophetic dreams from her deceased father-in-law warning of future events (fire, death), and the tzaddikim acknowledge she 'sees better than them.'
The Besht supernaturally knows of impending Sabbath desecration and removes/restores another's Torah knowledge, demonstrating supernatural perception and knowledge.
The Rav perceives via divine wisdom that Tsar Pavel is no longer truly Tsar and senses 'new things' (his assassination) before bells ring, demonstrating supernatural perception.

The story describes Rabbi Aharon's supernatural perception—'every secret was revealed to him'—and the Baal HaTanya recognizing the child's spiritual stature on sight, both clear instances of ruach hakodesh.
